text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
FoundationOne CDx (F1CDx) — Solid Tumor Panel

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PATIENT & SPECIMEN INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Patient Name:        Renata Okonkwo
Date of Birth:       [on file]
Sex:                 Female
Ordering Physician:  [Oncology Attending]
Institution:         [Medical Center]

Specimen ID:         [LAB-XXXX-4421]
Specimen Type:       Core needle biopsy, formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE)
Anatomic Site:       Right axillary lymph node
Date Collected:      [on file]
Date Received:       [on file]
Tumor Diagnosis:     Metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E-positive, Stage IV M1c
Tumor Purity (Estimated):  60% (adequate for analysis)
Percent Tumor Nuclei:      55% (pathologist-reviewed H&E)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
METHODOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
This report was generated using the FoundationOne CDx (F1CDx) assay, a next-generation sequencing (NGS)-based in vitro diagnostic device cleared by the FDA as a companion diagnostic. The assay interrogates the full coding sequence of 324 cancer-relevant genes, select introns of 36 genes for rearrangement detection, whole genome copy number analysis (shallow), and two genomic signatures (tumor mutational burden [TMB] and microsatellite instability [MSI]). Additionally, select genes were assessed at higher depth for variant detection using a hybrid capture-based approach, with a mean exon coverage of >500x. TERT promoter (c.-124 and c.-146 positions) is assessed by a targeted deep sequencing addon. PD-L1 expression (22C3 companion diagnostic) was performed as a separate immunohistochemical assay on the same specimen block.

A separate germline analysis was performed on a concurrently submitted peripheral blood sample using a targeted hereditary cancer panel (BRCA1, BRCA2, and 68 additional cancer predisposition genes; Myriad myRisk). Results are summarized in the Germline section below.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SOMAT IC ALTERATIONS — PATHOGENIC / LIKELY PATHOGENIC

  Gene        Alteration                        Allele Freq    Copy #    Classification
  -------     --------------------------------  -----------    ------    ----------------
  BRAF        p.V600E (c.1799T>A)               48%            —         Pathogenic, Tier I
  CDKN2A      p.Q95* (c.283C>T); nonsense       52%            —         Pathogenic, Tier II
  CDKN2B      Homozygous deletion               —              0         Pathogenic, Tier II
  PTEN        p.R130G (c.388C>G); missense      31%            —         Likely Pathogenic, Tier II
  NF1         Splice site c.1392-1G>A           22%            —         Likely Pathogenic, Tier II
  TP53        p.R248W (c.742C>T); missense      39%            —         Pathogenic, Tier II
  TERT        Promoter c.-124C>T                55%            —         Pathogenic, Tier II
  MCL1        Amplification                     —              8         Likely Pathogenic, Tier III
  MYC         Copy number gain                  —              5         Variant of Unknown Significance
  CDK4        Copy number gain                  —              4         Variant of Unknown Significance
  RAC1        p.P29S (c.85C>T); missense        18%            —         Likely Pathogenic, Tier II

GENES TESTED — NO REPORTABLE ALTERATION DETECTED

  NRAS — wild-type
  KIT  — wild-type
  MAP2K1 (MEK1) — wild-type
  MAP2K2 (MEK2) — wild-type
  GNAQ — wild-type
  GNA11 — wild-type
  NF2  — wild-type
  IDH1 / IDH2 — wild-type
  EGFR — wild-type
  ALK  — no rearrangement detected
  ROS1 — no rearrangement detected
  MET  — no amplification, no exon 14 skip
  NTRK1 / NTRK2 / NTRK3 — no fusion detected
  RET  — no rearrangement detected
  BRCA1 / BRCA2 (somatic) — wild-type
  MLH1 / MSH2 / MSH6 / PMS2 — no pathogenic alteration; microsatellite stable

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GENOMIC SIGNATURES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):     28 mutations per megabase (HIGH; threshold for high: >=10 mut/Mb)
Microsatellite Instability (MSI):  MSS (Microsatellite Stable)
PD-L1 Expression (22C3 IHC):       TPS 15% (Positive, low-to-intermediate expression)
                                   CPS not applicable (solid tumor, not urothelial/gastric)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GERMLINE ANALYSIS (PERIPHERAL BLOOD)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Assay: Myriad myRisk Hereditary Cancer Panel (70-gene panel)
Result: NO PATHOGENIC OR LIKELY PATHOGENIC GERMLINE VARIANTS IDENTIFIED

Genes of particular interest reviewed:
  BRCA1          — wild-type
  BRCA2          — wild-type
  CDKN2A (p16)   — no pathogenic germline variant (somatic origin of tumor CDKN2A alteration confirmed)
  BAP1           — wild-type
  MC1R           — not assessed on this panel; recommend separate assessment if clinically indicated
  MITF            — wild-type

Conclusion: No hereditary predisposition syndrome identified. The CDKN2A loss detected in tumor tissue is consistent with somatic, tumor-acquired alteration rather than germline FAMMM (Familial Atypical Multiple Mole Melanoma) syndrome.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DETAILED VARIANT INTERPRETATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

BRAF p.V600E (Tier I — Actionable)
The BRAF p.V600E substitution (valine-to-glutamate at codon 600) is the most common oncogenic mutation in cutaneous melanoma, occurring in approximately 50% of cases. This hotspot mutation results in constitutive kinase activation of the MAPK/ERK pathway independent of upstream RAS signaling. A variant allele frequency of 48% in this specimen is consistent with the estimated tumor purity and suggests a clonally dominant event. BRAF V600E is a companion diagnostic biomarker for FDA-approved BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib) and the dabrafenib/trametinib combination (Tafinlar/Mekinist), as well as encorafenib/binimetinib (Braftovi/Mektovi). This alteration is also identified on the FoundationOne CDx as a companion diagnostic for several approved agents.

CDKN2A p.Q95* / CDKN2B Homozygous Deletion (Tier II)
The CDKN2A gene at chromosome 9p21 encodes p16/INK4A, a critical negative regulator of CDK4/6 and the G1 cell-cycle checkpoint. The nonsense mutation p.Q95* results in premature termination and loss of p16 function. Co-deletion of CDKN2B (which encodes p15/INK4B) at allelic copy number of zero indicates homozygous deletion of the entire 9p21.3 locus, a pattern frequently seen in aggressive cutaneous melanoma. The concurrent CDKN2A nonsense mutation and CDKN2B co-deletion represent biallelic inactivation of the p16-CDK4/6-RB axis. This loss may confer sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitors (e.g., palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) in combination strategies, though clinical utility in melanoma remains investigational. CDK4 copy number gain (see below) further deregulates the G1 checkpoint.

PTEN p.R130G (Tier II — Likely Pathogenic, Loss of Function)
PTEN encodes a lipid phosphatase that antagonizes the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. The p.R130G missense substitution occurs at a critical catalytic residue within the phosphatase domain and is a recognized loss-of-function alteration. PTEN loss is found in approximately 20-30% of metastatic melanomas and is associated with constitutive AKT activation and resistance to MAPK pathway inhibition. The co-occurrence of PTEN loss with BRAF V600E in this specimen is noteworthy: pre-clinical and clinical data indicate that PTEN-null BRAF-mutant melanomas demonstrate reduced sensitivity to BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations, with PI3K pathway activation serving as a bypass mechanism. A variant allele frequency of 31% suggests possible subclonal heterogeneity or hemizygous loss at the PTEN locus.

NF1 Splice Site c.1392-1G>A (Tier II — Likely Pathogenic)
NF1 encodes neurofibromin, a GTPase-activating protein (GAP) that negatively regulates RAS by accelerating GTP hydrolysis. The intronic splice acceptor mutation c.1392-1G>A is predicted to disrupt canonical splicing and result in exon skipping or intron retention, leading to a truncated or functionally impaired neurofibromin. NF1 loss-of-function results in hyperactivation of RAS and downstream MAPK and PI3K signaling. In the context of this tumor, the co-mutation of NF1 (RAS activator) and BRAF V600E (direct MAPK activator) alongside PTEN loss creates a highly activated oncogenic signaling milieu. NF1 mutations are found in approximately 14% of cutaneous melanomas as drivers (so-called "NF1-mutant" melanoma subtype) but when co-occurring with BRAF V600E, as here, are more likely representing secondary/co-mutational events. Notably, the relatively lower VAF (22%) may indicate subclonal representation, though germline NF1 has been excluded.

TP53 p.R248W (Tier II — Pathogenic, Gain of Function)
TP53 is the most commonly mutated tumor suppressor gene in human cancer. The p.R248W hotspot mutation at codon 248 (arginine-to-tryptophan) is one of the most frequently observed TP53 gain-of-function mutations. Unlike loss-of-function TP53 mutations, p.R248W produces a mutant p53 protein that actively suppresses the transcriptional activity of wild-type p53 family members (p63, p73) and gains novel oncogenic transcriptional activities, promoting invasion, chemoresistance, and genomic instability. A VAF of 39% suggests clonal representation consistent with tumor purity. TP53 gain-of-function mutations are associated with more aggressive disease behavior and genomic instability, consistent with the elevated mitotic rate and high TMB observed in this specimen.

TERT Promoter c.-124C>T (Tier II)
The TERT promoter mutation c.-124C>T (also described as C228T in the promoter) creates a de novo ETS transcription factor binding motif, resulting in markedly elevated TERT expression and telomerase activity. TERT promoter mutations are found in approximately 70-75% of cutaneous melanomas and represent an early and near-universal driver event in this malignancy, contributing to replicative immortality. A high VAF of 55% in this specimen is consistent with clonal, likely early acquisition. No direct therapeutic targeting of TERT is currently clinically validated.

MCL1 Amplification (Tier III)
MCL1, encoding an anti-apoptotic BCL-2 family protein, is amplified (copy number 8) in this tumor. MCL1 amplification provides a mechanism of resistance to apoptosis and has been associated with resistance to cytotoxic chemotherapy and targeted agents. MCL1 inhibitors (e.g., AMG 176, S64315/MIK665) are under early-phase clinical investigation. CDK4 copy number gain (CN 4) further deregulates the G1 checkpoint in the setting of complete CDKN2A/2B loss.

RAC1 p.P29S (Tier II — Likely Pathogenic)
RAC1 p.P29S is a recurrent hotspot mutation found in approximately 5-9% of sun-exposed cutaneous melanomas. RAC1 is a member of the Rho GTPase family; the P29S substitution creates a constitutively activated, fast-cycling GTPase that promotes actin polymerization, cell motility, cell cycle entry, and melanocyte proliferation. This mutation is associated with ultraviolet-signature mutational patterns and may contribute to resistance to BRAF-targeted therapy through MEK-independent activation of downstream pathways.

Co-mutation Pattern and Resistance Implications
The co-occurrence of BRAF V600E with concurrent PTEN loss (p.R130G), NF1 splice-site inactivation, and RAC1 P29S in this tumor represents a molecularly complex landscape with multiple potential mediators of intrinsic and early acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy. Published data from multiple institutional cohorts and the COMBI-v/COMBI-d resistance analyses identify PTEN loss as a key driver of PI3K/AKT-mediated bypass of MAPK inhibition. NF1 co-mutation can maintain RAS-GTP levels sufficient to re-engage CRAF-dependent MEK activation despite BRAF inhibitor presence. These findings carry important clinical implications: while initial response to BRAF/MEK inhibition is expected, depth and durability of response may be attenuated compared to BRAF V600E-only tumors. Close monitoring for early progression is warranted.

High TMB (28 mut/Mb) and prior PD-L1 positivity (TPS 15%) are favorable predictors of potential response to immune checkpoint blockade, which should be considered as the next therapeutic strategy should targeted therapy fail.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
THERAPEUTIC ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tier I — FDA-Approved Therapies with Companion Diagnostic Indication (Same Tumor Type)
  BRAF p.V600E:
    - Dabrafenib (Tafinlar) + Trametinib (Mekinist) — FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K metastatic melanoma
    - Vemurafenib (Zelboraf) + Cobimetinib (Cotellic) — FDA-approved for BRAF V600E metastatic melanoma
    - Encorafenib (Braftovi) + Binimetinib (Mektovi) — FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K metastatic melanoma
    - Vemurafenib monotherapy — FDA-approved (less preferred given superior outcomes with combination)

  TMB-High (28 mut/Mb):
    - Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) — FDA-approved for TMB-high (>=10 mut/Mb) solid tumors (tumor-agnostic)

Tier II — Potential Benefit Based on Biological Rationale / Off-Label / Other Tumor Types
  PTEN Loss:
    - PI3K/AKT/mTOR inhibitors (e.g., everolimus, alpelisib, ipatasertib) — clinical trial setting in melanoma
    - Consideration in combination BRAF/MEK + PI3K inhibitor protocols
  CDKN2A/2B Loss + CDK4 Gain:
    - CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) — investigational in melanoma; clinical trials available
  MCL1 Amplification:
    - MCL1 inhibitors — early-phase clinical trials
  PD-L1 TPS 15%:
    - Nivolumab (Opdivo) — FDA-approved for unresectable/metastatic melanoma (1st or 2nd line)
    - Ipilimumab + Nivolumab — FDA-approved combination for unresectable/metastatic melanoma
    - Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) — FDA-approved for unresectable/metastatic melanoma

Tier III — Investigational / Clinical Trial Context Only
  RAC1 p.P29S — no approved therapy; targeted agents preclinical/early phase
  NF1 loss — MEK inhibitor sensitivity explored in NF1-driven melanoma; in BRAF-mutant co-mutant context, utility unclear
  TERT promoter — no approved therapy

NO REPORTABLE FUSIONS / REARRANGEMENTS DETECTED (NTRK, ALK, ROS1, RET): Targeted therapies for these alterations not indicated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
QUALITY METRICS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Mean exon coverage depth:        614x (PASS; threshold >100x)
Percent exons >100x coverage:    98.7% (PASS)
Sample quality score:            PASS
Tumor purity (estimated):        60% (PASS; threshold >20%)
Formalin fixation artifact:      Low (PASS)
Sequencing platform:             Illumina NovaSeq 6000

This report was reviewed and signed by a board-certified molecular pathologist.

This report is intended for use by qualified healthcare professionals in conjunction with other clinical, pathologic, and laboratory findings. The results should be interpreted in the context of this patient's complete clinical picture. Variants of unknown significance (VUS) are reported for informational purposes and should not be used as the sole basis for treatment decisions. Clinical trial enrollment should be considered where available.
